text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Niraparib Dose Modification / Drug Re-initiation Visit

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei is a 53-year-old postmenopausal female with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant) presenting for re-initiation of niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy following a planned 28-day drug hold for Grade 3 thrombocytopenia.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei was diagnosed with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis and omental caking approximately 12-14 months ago. She underwent optimal primary debulking surgery achieving R0 resection (TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node sampling), followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy. Paclitaxel was dose-reduced from 175 mg/m2 to 135 mg/m2 after Cycle 3 due to Grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy. She completed all 6 cycles and achieved a complete clinical and radiographic response, with CA-125 normalization to 11 U/mL and no evidence of disease on restaging CT.

Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, HRD-positive tumor, and complete response to platinum-based therapy, she was initiated on niraparib maintenance at 200 mg orally daily (individualized starting dose; weight 68 kg, baseline platelets 187 x10^9/L). Approximately 6 weeks into maintenance therapy, routine CBC monitoring revealed a trajectory of progressive thrombocytopenia: baseline platelets 187 x10^9/L prior to initiation, Week 2 platelets 134 x10^9/L (Grade 1), Week 4 platelets 62 x10^9/L (Grade 2), and Week 6 platelets 41 x10^9/L (Grade 3, platelets < 50 x10^9/L). No bleeding symptoms were reported at that time. Peripheral blood smear showed no evidence of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia or platelet clumping. Niraparib was held per package insert guidance.

During the 28-day hold period, Ms. Osei had serial platelet monitoring: Day 14 platelets 98 x10^9/L (recovering), Day 28 platelets 156 x10^9/L (fully recovered). No bleeding events occurred during the hold period. She reports that the interval without niraparib was well tolerated overall, though she did notice that the mild nausea and fatigue she had attributed to the drug improved somewhat during the hold.

She returns today for reassessment and planned re-initiation of niraparib at a reduced dose of 100 mg daily.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue Grade 1, improved compared to prior niraparib visit. No fever, no chills, no unintentional weight loss.
- Hematologic: No bruising, no petechiae, no bleeding from gums, no epistaxis, no hematuria, no melena or hematochezia during hold period.
- Neurologic: Residual bilateral toe tingling from prior paclitaxel-associated peripheral neuropathy, now improving - Grade 0 to 1. Fine motor tasks fully intact. No new neurologic symptoms.
- GI: Mild intermittent nausea, primarily in the morning, present on prior niraparib (taking medication at bedtime per instructions). Improved during the hold. Ondansetron used intermittently with benefit. No vomiting. Appetite intact. Bowel function normal.
- GYN/Genitourinary: Vaginal dryness managed with topical vaginal estrogen, no change. No pelvic pain, no vaginal bleeding.
- Cardiovascular: No palpitations, no chest pain, no exertional dyspnea. No hypertension symptoms.
- Musculoskeletal: No new joint pains or myalgias.
- Psychiatric: Good mood overall. Returning to full-time work as a high school biology teacher. Mild anxiety about recurrence, manageable.

---

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, Temp 98.5°F, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 68 kg (stable).
General: Alert and oriented x3, in no acute distress. Well-appearing woman.
Skin: No petechiae, no purpura, no bruising. No jaundice. No pallor.
HEENT: No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.
Cardiopulmonary: Regular rate and rhythm. Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or rubs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed, nontender. No palpable masses. No hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites by clinical exam.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Pulses intact bilaterally. No joint swelling.
Neurologic: Grossly intact. Sensation mildly decreased in bilateral toes (residual paclitaxel neuropathy, Grade 0-1), finger sensation and fine motor intact.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CBC (today):
  - WBC: 5.4 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1; stable from prior)
  - Platelets: 156 x10^9/L (recovered from Grade 3 nadir of 41 x10^9/L)
  - ANC: 3.1 x10^9/L (normal)
Comprehensive metabolic panel: BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), AST 22 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, alk phos 78 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL - all within normal limits.
CA-125: 12 U/mL (normalized, reference < 35 U/mL; stable, no evidence of biochemical recurrence).

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging ordered at this visit. Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis (approximately 4-5 weeks prior): No peritoneal implants, no ascites, no pelvic masses, no lymphadenopathy. Complete radiographic remission maintained.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO IIIC, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant - COMPLETE REMISSION:
   Ms. Osei remains in complete clinical and radiographic remission. CA-125 is stable at 12 U/mL, normalized since chemotherapy completion. CT imaging from recent surveillance shows no evidence of disease. She is doing extremely well from an oncologic standpoint.

2. GRADE 3 THROMBOCYTOPENIA ON NIRAPARIB - RESOLVED, DRUG HOLD COMPLETED:
   Platelet count has fully recovered to 156 x10^9/L after 28-day hold. The nadir was 41 x10^9/L at Week 6, consistent with known drug-induced suppression rather than a primary hematologic process (peripheral smear normal, no MAHA). No bleeding events occurred. Per standard niraparib prescribing guidance, she is eligible to resume at a dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily. This represents the lowest approved dose for this drug, and she was counseled that any further Grade 3+ thrombocytopenia at 100 mg would require permanent discontinuation. She understands and accepts this plan.

   PLAN: Resume niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily (1 x 100 mg capsule at bedtime). Continue weekly CBC for the first 4 weeks after resumption, then monthly thereafter if stable.

3. NAUSEA AND FATIGUE (GRADE 1) - ANTICIPATE RECURRENCE AT LOWER DOSE, LIKELY MORE TOLERABLE:
   She was counseled that mild nausea and fatigue may recur at 100 mg but are typically less pronounced than at 200 mg. Ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN remains prescribed; instructed to take niraparib at bedtime. Small frequent meals, avoidance of strong food odors. Will reassess at follow-up. No dose modification warranted for Grade 1 side effects.

4. RESIDUAL PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (GRADE 0-1) FROM PRIOR PACLITAXEL:
   Continuing to improve. Gabapentin was initiated at a low dose previously; patient reports only taking it occasionally and that symptoms are very mild. No changes needed. Expected to continue to resolve slowly.

5. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT:
   Topical vaginal estrogen and venlafaxine for hot flashes continued per prior plan. Local estrogen use consistent with gynecologic oncology guidance in this setting.

6. CANCER GENETICS / FAMILY:
   Patient reports that her 26-year-old daughter is in the process of pursuing BRCA1 germline testing following referral initiated at prior visit. Encouraged to ensure daughter follows up with genetic counseling team.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to clinic in 4 weeks for CBC check and clinical reassessment post-niraparib resumption. CA-125 to be rechecked in 3 months. Next surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3-4 months (approximately 6 months after chemotherapy completion).

Time spent: 45 minutes, greater than 50% counseling regarding drug re-initiation, toxicity monitoring, and ongoing oncologic surveillance.

---
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
